title: Git & GitHub Tutorial
body:
Git & GitHub Tutorial

Practical reference covering setup, cloning, committing, branching, restoring files, and CI/CD with GitHub Actions.

1. Connecting a New Project via HTTPS

Create an empty repo on GitHub (skip README/gitignore if your local folder already has files, to avoid a conflict on first push). Then inside your project folder:

  • git init
  • git add .
  • git commit -m "Initial commit"
  • git branch -M main
  • git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
  • git push -u origin main

Authentication note: GitHub no longer accepts your account password for HTTPS git operations. Git Credential Manager (bundled with Git for Windows) opens a browser login popup on first push and remembers you. Alternatively, generate a Personal Access Token under GitHub Settings, Developer settings, Personal access tokens, and paste it in place of your password when prompted.

2. Connecting to an Existing Project (Clone & Pull)

To clone a repo you don't have locally yet:

  • git clone https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
  • cd repo-name

To pull the latest changes into a repo you already have:

  • git pull origin main

If you already have a local folder not yet linked to the GitHub repo:

  • git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repo-name.git
  • git fetch origin
  • git checkout main

If your local folder has commits with no shared history, add --allow-unrelated-histories to the pull command.

3. Committing to GitHub

  • git status — see what's changed
  • git add <file> — stage a specific file
  • git add . — stage everything in the current directory
  • git commit -m "message" — commit staged changes
  • git push origin <branch> — push to GitHub

Commit message convention: feat: new feature, fix: bug fix, chore: maintenance/cleanup, docs: documentation only, refactor: code change that isn't a fix or feature, style: formatting only, test: adding or updating tests.

4. Branching

  • git checkout -b branch-name — create and switch in one step
  • git branch — list local branches
  • git checkout branch-name — switch to an existing branch
  • git push -u origin branch-name — push new branch, set upstream

Merge back via GitHub PR (recommended): push the branch, open a pull request into main, review the diff, then merge through the UI.

Or merge locally:

  • git checkout main
  • git pull origin main
  • git merge branch-name
  • git push origin main

Clean up after merge:

  • git branch -d branch-name
  • git push origin --delete branch-name

5. git restore

  • git restore <file> — discards uncommitted edits, reverts to last commit (also undoes an uncommitted deletion)
  • git restore --staged <file> — unstages a file, keeps the edits
  • git restore --source=<commit> <file> — restores a file's contents from a specific past commit

Only works on uncommitted changes. For already-committed/pushed changes, use git revert <commit> (safe for shared branches) or git reset (rewrites history, local-only commits).

Advanced: CI/CD with GitHub Actions

Workflows live in .github/workflows/*.yml. Basic example — lint and build on every push/PR to main:

  • on: push / pull_request, branches: [main]
  • jobs.build.runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  • steps: actions/checkout@v4
  • steps: actions/setup-node@v4 (node-version 20)
  • run: npm ci
  • run: npm run lint
  • run: npm run build

Secrets (API keys, DB URLs) go under repo Settings, Secrets and variables, Actions, referenced as ${{ secrets.YOUR_SECRET_NAME }} — never hardcoded in the YAML.

Branch protection (Settings, Branches) can require this workflow to pass before a PR merges into main, turning CI into an actual gate.

Deployment: on Vercel, connecting the GitHub repo auto-deploys every push and generates preview URLs per PR — no deploy step needed in Actions. For other hosts, add a final job gated to main using SSH/rsync/FTP actions, with needs: build so it waits for the build job to succeed first.
